coredata.dc:title: Amine-enriched Graphene Quantum Dots for High-pseudocapacitance Supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhen
Qin, Ping
Wang, Liang
Yang, Chengshuai
Li, Yanfeng
Chen, Zhiwen
Pan, Dengyu
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: The applications of carbon-based supercapacitors have been limited by their low energy storage density owing to their limited active storage sites. To overcome this limitation, amine-enriched porous carbon electrodes have been fabricated by the electrostatic fusion of amine-functionalized single-crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) within conductive, vertically ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays as the collectors. The carbon films deliver ultrahigh specific capacitance (400–595Fg−1) even beyond the theoretical upper limit of single-layer graphene by inducing a high concentration of active amine moieties at edge. Symmetrical GQD supercapacitors in H2SO4 electrolyte offer energy density up to 21.8Whkg−1 and retain 90% of the initial capacitance after 10000 cyclic voltammetry tests. The results show that amine-enriched GQDs can function as a new kind of highly active, solution-processable, and low-cost pseudocapacitive materials applicable to high-performance supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: graphene quantum dots
amine-functionalization
pseudocapacitance
supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top SEM images of GQD-TNA thin films deposited for 0h (a), 1h (b), 2h (c), and 3h (d). The lateral view (e), EDS spectrum taken at the middle region (f), and component maps (g–j) of Ti, O, C, and N across the film from top to bottom.
(a) Survey XPS spectra of TNAs, amine-GQDs, and amine-GQD-loaded TNAs. (b) Their Raman spectra.

Three-electrode electrochemical properties of pristine TNAs and GQD-TNA films. a) CV curves at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at a current density of 1mAcm−2. c) Areal capacitance vs scan rate. d) Areal capacitance of GQD-TNA films at 10mVs−1 vs electrodeposition time at different potentials.

Performance of symmetrical supercapacitors using amine-GQD-TNAs deposited for 1, 2, 3h as the electrodes. a) CV curves at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves. c) Specific capacitance vs current density. d) Capacitance retention and Coulombic efficiency of GQD-TNA films (3h) vs cycle number. e) Nyquist plots of the pristine and GQD-modified TNAs. f) Ragone plots.

head.ce:title: Amine-enriched Graphene Quantum Dots for High-pseudocapacitance Supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 201800, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The applications of carbon-based supercapacitors have been limited by their low energy storage density owing to their limited active storage sites. To overcome this limitation, amine-enriched porous carbon electrodes have been fabricated by the electrostatic fusion of amine-functionalized single-crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) within conductive, vertically ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays as the collectors. The carbon films deliver ultrahigh specific capacitance (400–595Fg−1) even beyond the theoretical upper limit of single-layer graphene by inducing a high concentration of active amine moieties at edge. Symmetrical GQD supercapacitors in H2SO4 electrolyte offer energy density up to 21.8Whkg−1 and retain 90% of the initial capacitance after 10000 cyclic voltammetry tests. The results show that amine-enriched GQDs can function as a new kind of highly active, solution-processable, and low-cost pseudocapacitive materials applicable to high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors store electrical energy typically through electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC) that provides high power density, fast charging rate, and long cycle life. Currently, their widespread applications are still limited by lower energy storage density compared to that of electrical cells [1–4]. Research has thus been focused on maximizing specific capacitance and/or improving electrochemical window of carbon-based electrode materials [1–20]. Among carbon materials, graphene is the most promising for supercapacitor applications because of exceptionally high surface area and electrical conductivity, as well as low-cost production and facile modification with other materials [1–3]. Ideal single-layer graphene, if its entire area could be used, would have EDLC capacitance up to 550Fg−1 [5], and thus store a maximal energy density of 19Whkg−1 (aqueous electrolyte). Practical graphene electrodes have to form a dense, cross-linked network to facilitate charge transfer, which in turn reduces their surface area accessible for electrolyte ions. Moreover, single-layer graphene sheets tend to restack and aggregate through interlayer interaction. Consequently, it remains a great challenge to increase energy storage density in green aqueous supercapacitors using graphene materials with far lower EDLC capacitance than the theoretical value [1,2,5–14].

Pseudocapacitive materials hold the potential for the next generation of supercapacitors with higher energy density, since they can provide both surface and internal active sites in large amounts for reversible charge storage. Metal oxides (e.g., RuO2, MnO2, and NiO) [21–23] and conducting polymers (e.g., polyaniline, polypyrrole, and polythiophene) [24–26] are conventional pseudocapacitive materials capable of accommodating much higher specific capacitance and energy storage density than any carbon materials alone. Nevertheless, the enhanced energy density is compromised with short cycle life and low power density owing to their poor conductivity, sluggish and less reversible redox reactions, and intrinsic structural degradation during the redox process.

Functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have currently aroused increasing attention because of a myriad of alluring physicochemical properties and a wide range of potential applications [27–36]. GQDs are usually prepared by hash chemical or electrochemical cutting protocols that produce highly defective GQDs with oxygen-containing moieties randomly distributed at the basal plane and edge sites [27–29]. Because of the defective structure, poor physiochemical properties, difficulty in control over size and functionality, and production in low amounts, the fundamental studies and widespread applications of GQDs are limited. Recently, we have developed a molecular fusion strategy to mass produce single-crystalline GQDs with excellent optical properties [30]. Moreover, surface functionalization with various groups can be easily achieved in situ and exclusively at edge sites. It is thus expected to construct high-performance supercapacitors using well-functionalized single-crystalline GQDs with rich redox sites. Here we report that amine-functionalized single-crystalline GQDs incorporated within vertically ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) can deliver ultrahigh specific capacitance (400–595Fg−1) even beyond the theoretical upper limit of single-layer graphene by inducing a high concentration of active amine moieties at edge. We found that symmetrical GQD supercapacitors in H2SO4 electrolyte can offer energy density up to 21.8Whkg−1 and retain 90% of the initial capacitance after 10000 cyclic voltammetry tests. This finding suggests that amine-enriched GQDs can function as a new kind of highly active, solution-processable, and low-cost pseudocapacitive materials applicable to high-performance supercapacitors.

Preparation of materials: The amine-GQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal molecular fusion method [30]. In a typical procedure, pyrene (2g, TCI, purity >98%) was nitrated into trinitropyrene in hot HNO3 (160ml) at 80°C under refluxing and stirring for 12h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized (DI) water (1L) and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the acid. The resultant yellow 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (3.0g, yield of 90%) was dispersed in a hydrazine hydrate solution of DI water (0.6L, 1.5M) by ultrasonication (500W, 40kHz) for 2h. The suspension was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (1L) and heated at 200°C for 10h. After cooled to room temperature, the product containing the amine-GQDs was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon product.

Conductive TNAs were prepared by second anodic oxidization and annealing in vacuum [37]. Prior to the oxidation, titanium foils (15×18mm2) were polished in a mixture of hydrofluoric acid and nitric acid for 1min (HF/HNO3/H2O=1:4:5 in volume). After rinsed with DI water, the Ti foils were further cleaned by ultrasonication in acetone, ethanol and deionized (DI) water successively before drying with nitrogen stream. The first anodization of the Ti foils as anodes was carried out at 50V DC potential in a home-made well-sealed two-electrode cell at room temperature using graphite sheets as cathodes. The electrolyte consisted of ethylene glycol containing 0.4wt.% NH4F and 5wt.% of DI water. After 2h oxidation, the first-grown inhomogeneous TNA membrane was removed by ultrasonication in DI water for several minutes, leaving a hexagonally packed pattern of regular shallow pits on the Ti foil surface. The second anodization was then performed under the same conditions as the first anodization, and the hexagonally packed pattern served as a template to guide the vertical growth of nanotubes underneath during the second oxidation. The as-grown amorphous TNAs were rinsed with DI water for several times and dried with nitrogen stream. To induce crystallization into the anatase phase, the amorphous TNAs were calcined in vaccum at 450°C for 2h at a heating rate of 0.5°Cmin−1 in a CVD system.

GQDs in aqueous solution (30mL, 1200mgL−1, pH: 12) were electrodeposited within conductive TNAs at a constant voltage of 3V. In the electrodeposition system, TNAs and a platinum wire were set as the cathode and the anode, respectively. After the deposition, the TNAs loaded with GQDs was washed with DI water, dried in an oven,and annealed at 150°C for 3h.

Material characterization. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku D/max-2200 using Cu Kα radiation. SEM images were taken with JEOL FESEM-6700F under voltage of 15KV. XPS spectra were collected using Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc=785nm.

Electrochemical measurements. All electrochemical tests were carried out using an electrochemical working station (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) at room temperature. Under a three-electrode mode, GQD-modified TNAs were used as work electrode, a Ag/AgCl electrode (1M KCl) as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were measured within a potential window from −0.2 to 0.8V in 1M H2SO4 at scan rates varying from 5 to 100mVs−1. The galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were carried out from −0.2 to 0.8V in 1M H2SO4 at different current densities. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in the frequency range from 0.01 to 106Hz at open circuit potential with 5mV ac amplitude.

Two-electrode sandwich-type cells were fabricated using a stainless coin cell (2032) with a separator (NKK,TF4840, Japan) and 1M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte between the electrodes. GQD-modified TNAs (1.13cm2) were used as the two electrodes with nearly equal GQD weight. CV tests were measured in a potential window from 0 to 1.0V at scan rates varying from 5 to 100mVs−1. Galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements were measured at different current densities. Cycling stability tests were conducted on a Land CT2001A battery program-control test system (LAND, Wuhan, China) by galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements with a constant current density of 10Ag−1 for 10000 cycles.

In the three-electrode system, the specific capacitance was calculated according to the equation: C=IΔt/mΔV, where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time, m is the weight of the ractive material, and ΔV is the discharge voltage. In the two-electrode system, the specific capacitance was calculated according to the equation: C=2(IΔt)/(mΔV), where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time, m is the weight of the active material in the individual electrode, and ΔV is the discharge voltage. The energy density (E, Whkg−1) and the power density (P, kW kg−1) for a supercapacitor cell were calculated using the following equations [38]:
(1) E=0.5CTV2/3.6
(2) P=E×3600/t
where CT in Fg−1 is the specific capacitance of the supercapacitor cell, V in V is the voltage change during the discharge process after IR drop, and t in s is the discharge time.

The amine-enriched GQDs (2.9±0.5nm) were synthesized on a gram scale through a hydrothermal molecular fusion process in the presence of hydrazine hydrate (NH2NH2) using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as the molecular precursor, as previously reported [30]. They exhibit a nearly single-layer single-crystalline graphene structure with edge sites functionalized by amine and hydroxyl moieties with incorporated N content of 16.91at%. The TEM and HRTEM images (Supporting Information, Fig. S1) show that the GQDs are well dispersed with a spacing of 0.21nm corresponding to that of graphene (100) planes. Conductive TNAs were grown on Ti foil by anodization in ethylene glycol containing 5wt% water, and annealed in vacuum for crystallization [37]. Second anodization was adopted to improve TNA surface roughness, as shown in Fig. 1 a. A high concentration of oxygen vacancies is induced through the poor-oxygen annealing, leading to a large enhancement in electrical conductivity (two orders of magnitude higher than annealed in air [37]). The TNAs are crystallized preferentially along the c axis to form highly textured porous films (Supporting Information, Fig. S2), which facilitate both charge and ion transport within the films. The high conductivity of the TNAs not only favors the homogeneous electrophoretic deposition of the amine-GQDs within the TNAs to form amine-modified porous carbon films but also improves the electrical transport between the Ti collector and the carbon films separated by the TNAs for supercapacitor applications. The porous carbon films were facilely fabricated by electrophoretic deposition of the GQDs dispersed in alkaline aqueous solutions into conductive TNAs. To ensure the formation of the thin carbon films throughout the whole nanotube wall without blocking up the mouths of the nanotubes, random aggregation was excluded by a slow deposition process. Given that the GQDs are highly water-soluble in alkaline aqueous solution but tend to aggregate in acidic solution, the alkaline deposition can prevent the GQDs from random aggregation on the surface of the TNAs. In strongly alkaline solutions (pH=12), the amine-GQDs are highly negatively-charged, as exhibited by the negative Zeta (ζ) potential of the GQDs at pH=12 (Supporting Information, Fig. S3). Positive potentials were thus applied to deposit the negatively-charged GQDs into the TNAs with a controllable deposition rate. When a high GQD concentration (1.2gL−1) was used at 3V, different loads of the GQDs (0.345, 0.698, and 0.996mg) were deposited on a footprint area of 1.13cm−2 for durations of 1, 2, and 3h, respectively. Top SEM examination (Fig. 1b–d) shows that with increasing deposition duration or load weight, the tube wall becomes thicker, and the pore size smaller. For the 3h deposition, the pores shrink more markedly, but still keep open and allow electrolyte liquid to penetrate into their interior. For a longer deposition time, however, carbon spheres composed of fused GQDs form on the surface of the films, and the blockage of pores becomes obvious (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). Ideal deposition processes are controlled in 1–3h for 3V, and annealing at 150°C is required to stabilize the amine-functionalized porous carbon films in aqueous electrolyte. The cross-section view of the GQD-loaded TNAs (Fig. 1e) shows the tube length of 18μm. The EDS analysis shows the presence of Ti, O, C, and N elements (Fig. 1f). Elemental mapping images show a relatively homogeneous distribution from top to bottom for all the four elements (Fig. 1g–j), confirming that the amine-enriched GQDs are assembled onto the whole internal wall of the TNAs to form the porous carbon films.

The composition of the GQD-loaded TNAs is further analyzed by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum of the loaded TNAs shows enhanced signals of C 1s and N 1s compared with that of the empty TNAs (Fig. 2 a). The atomic contents of C, N, O, and Ti in the loaded sample, determined by the XPS analysis, are 73.9%, 13.8%, 2.2%, and 10.1%, respectively. The low contents of O and Ti are related to the limitation of the XPS analysis to shallow sample surface, where the deposited carbon layer is detected. The high-resolution N1s spectrum reveals the signals of amine at 399.4eV and N-C at 401.4eV (Supporting Information, Fig. S5), which is consistent with the previous observation in pure amine-GQDs [30]. The Raman spectra of the composite films, the GQDs, and the empty TNAs are compared (Fig. 2b). The empty TNAs exhibit the D and G peaks of carbon at 1377, and 1590cm−1, respectively. The carbon formation in the empty TNAs results from the vacuum pyrolysis of absorbed ethylene glycol. For the composite films, besides the strong D and G peaks, there is a weak but discernible additional peak between the two peaks. This unique Raman feature always exists in the OH-functionalized single-crystalline GQDs synthesized by the molecular fusion route [30], but not in defective GQDs prepared by all cutting routes [27–29]. Although the physical origin of the unusual Raman vibration is still unknown, it should be associated with the intrinsic structure of the molecularly fused GQDs rather than surface groups. The observation of the additional peak also confirms the presence of the GQDs within the TNAs.

Electrochemical measurements of the amine-modified porous carbon films within the TNAs as the work electrode were performed in a three-electrode system using Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and 1M H2SO4 as the electrolyte. For comparison, the electrochemical properties of pristine TNAs were also measured in the three-electrode system. Fig. 3 a shows the CV curves of the two electrodes. Like previous reports [37,39], the CV curve of the unmodified TNAs displays a rectangular shape, indicating of a EDLC behavior. In contrast, the CV curve of the porous carbon films exhibits a nonrectangular shape with three pairs of Faradic redox peaks observed in the potential range of 0.1–0.4V, suggesting a predominant pseudocapacitative feature. The porous carbon films show much larger area enclosed by the CV curve compared to the unloaded TNAs, indicating that the porous carbon arrays are highly Faraday-active. The greatly enhanced activity is also exhibited in the galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the two samples (Fig. 3b). The unloaded arrays show a triangle EDLC charging-discharging curve with a very short discharging duration of 25s, whereas the discharging curve of the porous carbon arrays exhibits a discharging process as long as 400s. The areal capacitance of the loaded arrays determined by the discharge curve is 428mFcm−2 at a scan rate of 5mVs−1, and remains 311mFcm−2 at 100mVs−1. The areal capacitance is enhanced by more than 20 folds compared to that of the unloaded TNAs sample (Fig. 3c). The electrochemical properties of the porous carbon films were found to be dependent on deposition potential and time. Fig. 3d shows the areal capacitance as a function of deposition time at potentials from 1 to 4V. Increasing the potential is able to achieve a high areal capacitance at a faster deposition rate. For example, at 4V for 1h deposition, the areal capacitance is up to 312mFcm−2. However, by extending the 4V deposition to longer time, there is only a slight increase in the areal capacitance. A SEM examination shows that the surface of the TNAs is covered with a layer of carbon microspheres after 4h deposition (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). These carbon microspheres contribute little to the overall areal capacitance owing to their low specific surface area. An optimized deposition potential is 3V, at which the areal capacitance increases to 428–526mFcm−2 for the 3–4h deposition.

Since the GQDs are co-functionalized by amine and hydroxyl, the redox activity of amine and hydroyl and their contribution to the pseudocapacitance in the porous carbon films is evaluated. To investigate the redox activity of hydroyl groups, single-crystalline GQDs functionalized exclusively by OH groups (OH-GQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal fusion of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in a NaOH solution [30], and then deposited electrophoretically into conductive TNAs grown at the same batch. The CV curve of the TNAs modified by OH-GQDs exhibits pseudocapacitance in the widow of −0.2 to 0.8V with redox peaks at 0.1-0.2V (Supporting Information, Fig. S7a). The areal capacitance of OH-GQD loaded TNAs with a load of 0.35mg is 25.5mFcm−2, much lower than that (207mFcm−2) of amine-GQD modified TNAs with a less load (0.33mg) (Supporting Information, Fig. S7b). This contrast indicates that amine is much more electrochemically active than hydroxyl. Therefore, the high areal capacitance of the amine-enriched GQDs mainly results from the reversible Faraday reaction of amine with H+ as shown in the equation:
>CNHNH2 ⇔>CNNH2 +H+ +e−

The performance of symmetrical GQD-based pseudosupercapacitors is evaluated in a two-electrode system using 1M H2SO4 as the electrolyte. The symmetrical supercapacitors were assembled using electrode samples (1.13cm2) containing the amine-GQDs of 0.350/0.394mg, 0.544/0.550mg, and 0.850/0.810mg prepared at 3V for 1, 2, and 3h, respectively, and the weight of TiO2 nanotube arrays was 2.2mg. The CV curves in these devices show much higher current density from 0 to 0.5V than in the range of 0.5–1.0V, which is consistent with a high pseudocapacitative activity of amine groups in the lower potentials (Fig. 4 a). The CV-enclosed area increases with inceasing GQD weight or deposition time. When the scan rate increases to 1000mVs−1, the CV curve basically maintains the shape, similar to that observed at 5mVs−1, indicating that the charging/discharging process is remarkably fast on the entire electrode surface even at a high scan rate (Supporting Information, Fig. S8). Their galvanostatic discharge curves at 1Ag−1 exhibit nearly symmetrical yet nonlinear characteristics without an obvious IR drop, suggesting a rapid and reversible Faradic reaction between H+ and surface amine (Fig. 4b). According to the discharge curves, the specific capacitance of the electrodes normalized on the weights of the loaded GQDs is 595, 508, and 400Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, respectively (The overall capacitance is ascribed to the loaded GQDs). Upon increasing the current density by 20 times (20Ag−1), their specific capacitance remains high (460, 368, and 238Fg−1), suggesting the excellent rate capability of the porous carbon films (Fig. 4c). Specific capacitances based on the total mass of the electrodes are shown in Fig. S9. The cyclic stability of the GQD-based pseudosupercapacitors was also evaluated for consecutive charge-discharge cycles. Even at a high constant current density of 10Ag−1, the capacitance can be stablized at 510Fg−1 with a 90% retantion of the initial capacitance after 10000 cycles (Fig. 4d). This excellent cylic stability is unusual for the pseudocapacitative reactions occurring within the less conductive TNA arrays as the collectors. The Coulombic effeciency can keep over 99%, further confirming a high reversiblity of the Faradic reaction between H+ and surface amine in the fused GQDs. Fig. 4e shows the Nyquist plots of the pure and GQD-modified TNAs. The equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the two electrodes are determined by the intersection of the curves at the X-axis, as shown in the inseted Nyquist plots in the high frequency response. The lower ESR (1Ω) of the GQD-modified TNAs than that of the pure TNAs (1.8Ω), indicates that the incorporation of the GQDs into the TNAs can greatly improve the electron and ion transport/diffusion in the device. Fig. 4f shows the Ragone plots of the three symmetrical devices. The low-load supercapacitor (1h) has an energy density as high as 21.8Whkg−1 at a power density of 0.25kWkg−1, approaching the lead acid batteries (26 to 34Whkg−1). Even for the higher-load supercapacitors (2h, 3h), the energy density remains higher than that of usual EDLC devices. The maximum power density can arrive at 25kWkg−1 at energy density of 10.8Whkg−1. These results indicate that the GQD-based pseudosupercapacitors have both high energy and power densities without sacrificing cycling life. A real device application is demonstrated in lighting red and green LED lights (the working potential of 2 and 3V, respectively) for more than 2minutes by using three and four tandem supercapacitors charged at 1mAcm−2 (Supporting Information, Fig. S10).

GQD-based micro-supercapacitors were reported to show a rather low areal capacitance of 0.53–0.67mFcm−2 at 15μAcm−2 [31,32]. GQD–carbon nanotube hybrid arrays for supercapacitors showed an areal capacitance of only 44mFcm−2 at 700μAcm−2 though their thickness was 5mm [33]. GQD-based aerogel was reported to show a high specific capacitance of 268Fg−1 at the current density of 1.25Ag−1 [34]. Compared with these previous reports on the GQD-based supercapacitors, the GQD-loaded TNAs with a thickness of 18μm show much higher areal capacitance and specific capacitance. For example, the areal capacitance of the GQD-loaded TNAs measured in the symmetrical aqueous system is 194mFcm−2 at 0.2mAcm−2, two orders of magnitude higher than that of the micro-supercapacitors and four times that of the much thicker GQDs/CNT hybrid arrays (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). The specific capacitance of the GQD-loaded TNAs at the current density of 1Ag−1 is up to 595Fg−1, about two folds more than that of the GQD-based aerogel [34]. This value represents the best result reported to date in all pure-carbon supercapacitors [6,7], even beyond the theoretical upper limit of ideal graphene capacitance (550Fg−1) [5]. For example, nearly ideal electrodes made of ultrathin planar graphene showed a specific capacitance of 247.3Fg−1 in aqueous electrolytes [6], merely 41.6% of that in the GQD-loaded TNAs. The specific capacitance of the N-enriched porous carbon films is also higher than that of functionalized graphene (417Fg−1 at 0.05Ag−1) [15], N-doped 3D graphene framework (484Fg−1 at 1Ag−1) [16] and nitrogen-enriched porous carbon nanosheets prepared by high-temperature thermal treatment (424Fg−1 at 0.1Ag−1) [17].

In conclusion, amine-enriched porous carbon films have been fabricated by the electrostatic fusion of the amine-functionalized single-crystalline GQDs within conductive TNAs. The carbon films exhibit a predominant pseudocapacitative behavior owing to the introduction of rich nitrigen active sites, which enable them to provide ultrahigh specific capaciance up to 400–595Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 for carbon-based ultracapacitors. Symmetrical supercapacitors constructed from the highlly pseudocapacitative but conductive carbon electrodes show combined merits of high energy density, high power density, and a long cycling life. It is highly expected that the next generation of high-performance carbon-based supercapacitors can be designed rationally and fabricated facilely by the incorporation of mass produced, solution-processable, and highlly pseudocapacitative amine-enriched GQDs into graphene 3D structures with a high EDLC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471098), Shanghai Natural Science Fund Project (No. 16ZR1412100), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.05.030.

TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images of amine-functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots, XRD patterns of TNAs and amine-GQD-TNAs, Zeta potential of the amine-GQDs versus pH of aqueous solutions, SEM image of the GQD-TNAs deposited at 3V for 4h and 4V for 4h, high-resolution Ti2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra of the amine-GQD-TNAs, CV curve of OH-GQD-TNAs and areal capacitances of the OH-GQD-TNAs and amine--GQD-TNAs at different scan rates, cyclic votammograms of GQD-loaded TNAs measured at scan rates of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000mVs−1, specific capacitances based on the total mass of the electrode vs current density deposited for 1, 2, 3h as the electrodes, photographs of red and green LEDs powered by the tandem GQD-based supercapacitors, areal capacitance of the GQD-loaded TNAs measured in the symmetrical aqueous system versus current density.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An Overview of the Applications of Graphene-Based Materials in Supercapacitors
Carbon-based supercapacitors produced by activation of graphene
Measurement of the quantum capacitance of graphene
Graphene-based supercapacitor with an ultrahigh energy density
Splitting of a vertical multiwalled carbon nanotube carpet to a graphene nanoribbon carpet and its use in supercapacitors
Three-dimensional strutted graphene grown by substrate-free sugar blowing for high-power-density supercapacitors
Thermal treatment effects on charge storage performance of graphene-based materials for supercapacitors
High-Perfor mance Multifun ctional Graphene Yarns: Toward Wearable All-Carbon Energy Storage Textiles
Vertically Oriented Graphene Bridging Active Layer/Current −Collector Interface for Ultrahigh Rate Supercapacitors
Flexible and conductive nanocomposite electrode based on graphene sheets and cotton cloth for supercapacitor
Bioinspired Effective Prevention of Restacking in Multilayered Graphene Films: Towards the Next Generation of High-Performance Supercapacitors
All-Graphene Core-Sheath Microfibers for All-Solid-State,Stretchable Fibriform Supercapacitors and Wearable Electronic Textiles
Renewing Functionalized Graphene as Electrodes for HighPerformance Supercapacitors
A Versatile, Ultralight: Nitrogen-Doped Graphene Framework
Graphene Coupled Schiff-base Porous Polymers: Towards Nitrogen-enriched Porous Carbon Nanosheets with Ultrahigh Electrochemical Capacity
